What's the best way to handle row-level security in my new app?

You should use Row Level Security (RLS) policies to control access on a row-by-row basis. By enabling RLS on your tables, you can ensure that users only interact with data they actually own. It's the gold standard for keeping your backend secure without writing complex middleware.

Does Supabase provide an API for my database automatically?

Yes, it does! Supabase provides an automatically generated REST API for your database, so you don't have to build one from scratch. When you use our SDKs, these requests are sent with the user's Auth Token to handle security through RLS.

How does Supabase handle real-time updates?

We use a Realtime Server that extends Postgres to let you listen for database changes as they happen. You can subscribe to a channel and get broadcast data or sync user states across your clients instantly. It's perfect for building chat apps or live dashboards.

What kind of tokens does Supabase Auth use for authentication?

Supabase Auth uses JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) to verify who your users are. These tokens integrate directly with PostgreSQL's RLS, making it easy to authorize access to specific resources. You can check all the JWT fields in the reference docs if you need the technical details.

How do I add more functionality like full-text search to my database?

Postgres is highly extensible, so you just need to enable the right extensions. For example, you can use the pg_cron extension to schedule recurring jobs or PostGIS for geo-queries. You can enable these with a single click in the Supabase dashboard.

Can I use external tools to manage my Supabase database?

Absolutely, since every project is a full Postgres database, you have standard level access. You can use tools like pgAdmin, DBeaver, or even the psql command-line tool. Just grab your connection string from the settings and you're good to go!

Where does Supabase store user data for authentication?

Auth data is stored in a special schema within your project's Postgres database. You can connect this information to your own tables using foreign key references or even triggers. This setup allows for a seamless integration between your app's data and user management.

What's the purpose of Supabase Storage?

Storage is designed to handle large files like images or videos that don't belong inside a database table. It's fully integrated with your Postgres database, meaning you can use RLS policies to control who can view or upload files. It makes serving media a breeze while keeping everything secure.

What are Edge Functions used for?

Edge Functions are server-side functions that run globally, as close to your users as possible to reduce latency. They're great for executing custom logic, like processing a payment or sending an email, without needing a full server. They integrate perfectly with the rest of the Supabase ecosystem.

How do I ensure referential integrity in my schema?

You should use foreign key constraints to link your tables together. This ensures that the relationships between your data stay consistent, like making sure a comment always belongs to an existing post. Postgres handles these checks automatically for you.

Is there a way to write raw SQL in the Supabase dashboard?

Yes, Supabase comes with a built-in SQL Editor where you can run queries and manage your database objects. You can even save your favorite snippets to reuse them later. It's a powerful tool for senior devs who prefer the command-line feel in a browser.

What does MVCC mean in the context of Postgres?

MVCC stands for Multiversion Concurrency Control, which lets multiple users read and write to the database at the same time without locking each other out. Each transaction sees a 'snapshot' of the data, keeping everything consistent and following ACID principles. It's one of the reasons Postgres is so stable under heavy workloads.

What are the ACID properties you mentioned?

ACID refers to Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. These properties ensure that your database transactions are processed reliably and that data remains accurate even in the event of a crash. Postgres has built-in support for these to keep your data safe.

Is Supabase hard to use if I'm not a database expert?

Not at all! Our table view makes Postgres as easy to use as a spreadsheet, so you can start building without being a SQL pro. You can manage tables, views, and data through a simple UI while still having the full power of Postgres underneath.

What's the difference between a view and a materialized view?

A standard view runs its query every time you call it, while a materialized view actually stores the result on disk. This makes materialized views much faster for complex queries that don't need to be perfectly up-to-the-second. You can refresh them whenever you need to update the cached data.

Who started the project that eventually became PostgreSQL?

Michael Stonebraker led the POSTGRES project at UC Berkeley starting in 1985. He actually won the Turing Award in 2014 for his work on these systems! It's incredible to think that the foundations of modern databases were built by such a pioneer.

When did the POSTGRES project first get published?

Published papers describing the system's basis started appearing in 1986. A prototype was later shown at the 1988 ACM SIGMOD Conference, showing off the ability to define custom types and relationships. It was a massive leap forward for database research at the time.

Why was the project renamed to PostgreSQL in 1996?

The name was changed in 1996 to reflect the database's support for the SQL query language. Before that, it was just called POSTGRES, referring to its roots as a successor to the Ingres database. It's been the industry standard for SQL compliance ever since.

I heard people use 'Postgres' and 'PostgreSQL' interchangeably. Why is that?

After a review in 2007, the development team decided to keep the official name as PostgreSQL but recognized 'Postgres' as a common alias. Most people find Postgres easier to say, so we use it for simplicity here at Supabase too. Both refer to the same advanced open-source database engine.

What was 'Postgres95'?

Postgres95 was created in 1994 when graduate students Andrew Yu and Jolly Chen replaced the original POSTQUEL interpreter with one for SQL. They announced the first beta in May 1995. This was a critical step in turning the Berkeley project into the SQL-compliant database we use today.

What language is the Postgres core written in?

The core of Postgres is written in C, which gives it incredible performance and stability. It also uses C++ for certain dependencies like LLVM. Most of its built-in functions are implemented in C to ensure they run as fast as possible.

When did the open-source effort first get its own development server?

The first non-university development server was provided by Marc Fournier on July 8, 1996. This marked the beginning of a global community effort to stabilize and grow the project. It's been maintained by the PostgreSQL Global Development Group ever since.

What is 'libpq'?

Libpq is the official C application programmer interface for PostgreSQL. It's a set of library functions that allow client programs to pass queries to the backend server and receive the results. It serves as the foundation for many other language-specific drivers.

Tell me a bit about the history of pgAdmin.

The first prototype, called pgManager, was written back in 1998 for Postgres 6.3.2. It evolved through several versions, and pgAdmin 4 was eventually released in 2016 with a Python-based backend. It's still one of the most popular GUIs for managing Postgres databases today.

What was the 'monitor' program used for?

The 'monitor' console was an early tool used to interact with the database before it was replaced by 'psql'. This happened during the transition to Postgres95. Psql added more features like tab completion and meta-commands to make our lives as developers much easier.

Pop quiz! What's the main difference between authentication and authorization?

Authentication is about checking that a user is who they say they are, like verifying a password. Authorization is about checking what resources that user is allowed to access, which we handle using RLS. You need both to build a truly secure application!

Do you remember which university the POSTGRES project started at?

It all started at the University of California, Berkeley back in the mid-80s. Michael Stonebraker returned there to address problems with the database systems of that era. It's amazing how much of modern tech still traces its roots back to that research.

Quick check: Name three core products Supabase provides besides the database.

Beyond the database, we offer Auth for user management, Storage for large files, and Edge Functions for serverless logic. We also have Realtime functionality to keep your apps synced. That's a solid stack for any modern developer!

Does a standard Supabase database backup include everything in my Storage buckets?

Actually, no. Database backups only include the metadata about your files, not the actual objects stored via the Storage API. If you restore an old backup, you won't get back files that were deleted since then, so plan your recovery strategy accordingly!

What's the name of the default schema created in every new database?

The default schema is called 'public'. While it's the standard place for your tables, you can create other schemas to organize your objects or manage permissions. Just remember that the public schema isn't mandatory, even though it's used by default.

Which index method is used by default for most table columns?

Postgres uses regular B-tree indexes by default, which are great for most sorting and comparison tasks. It also supports other methods like GiST, GIN, and BRIN for more specialized data types. Choosing the right index can make a massive difference in query performance!

Can you list three social auth providers Supabase supports?

Sure thing! We support popular options like Google, GitHub, and Discord, among many others. You can also integrate more specialized providers like Spotify or Slack depending on your app's needs. It makes onboarding a lot smoother for your users.

What kind of license does PostgreSQL use?

PostgreSQL is released under its own free and open-source PostgreSQL License, which is very permissive. It allows you to use, modify, and distribute the code for any purpose. This freedom is a huge part of why the Postgres ecosystem is so vibrant and diverse.

True or false: You have to be a database expert to enable extensions in Supabase.

False! You can enable Postgres extensions with the click of a button right in the Supabase dashboard. We've made it simple to expand your database's capabilities without needing to be a senior DBA. It's all about making powerful tools accessible to everyone.

Earlier, you mentioned Supabase was an alternative to Firebase. How do they compare when it comes to the database?

While Firebase uses a NoSQL document store, Supabase gives you a full relational PostgreSQL database. This means you get all the power of SQL, joins, and complex queries, which are often much more flexible for scaling. Plus, you're not locked into a proprietary platform since Postgres is open-source.

Last time we talked about RLS. How do I actually write a policy to restrict access?

You can use the 'CREATE POLICY' command to define your rules. For example, 'CREATE POLICY user_policy ON profiles FOR SELECT USING (auth.uid() = id);' would limit users to only seeing their own profile. It's a clean way to handle security directly at the data layer.

I'm practicing writing a Postgres function. Any tips for a senior developer?

Stick with PL/pgSQL for most tasks since it's safe and resembles other procedural languages like Oracle's PL/SQL. If you need ultimate performance, you could use C, but bugs there can crash the whole database. Always keep security in mind and use 'SECURITY DEFINER' carefully!

You mentioned JWTs before. How do they actually work with the Supabase client?

When a user signs in, the Supabase SDK automatically handles the JWT for you. Every time you make a data request, the token is sent along to prove the user's identity. This allows Postgres to use that identity when evaluating your RLS policies, keeping the whole process seamless and secure.

How would I migrate my existing app from Heroku to Supabase?

We have specific guides to help you migrate your data and auth from Heroku with minimal friction. Since Heroku already uses Postgres, you can often just export your schema and data directly into your new Supabase project. It's a great move for getting more features like Realtime and Storage out of the box.

Can you explain how the storage buckets work again?

Think of buckets as top-level containers for your files, similar to folders. You can create different buckets for public images versus private documents, and then apply RLS policies to each one. This gives you fine-grained control over who can access what, directly from your database logic.

How do I subscribe to a specific table for realtime changes?

You can use the 'supabase.channel()' method to subscribe to changes on a specific table. Just specify the table name and the type of event you're interested in, like inserts or updates. It's an incredibly efficient way to keep your UI in sync without constant polling.

What's the relationship between PostgREST and the Supabase API?

Supabase uses PostgREST under the hood to automatically generate a RESTful API from your Postgres schema. It's what allows you to perform CRUD operations directly from your client without writing any backend code. It's fast, secure, and incredibly developer-friendly!

When should I use an Edge Function instead of a database trigger?

Triggers are perfect for logic that stays inside the database, like updating a timestamp. Edge Functions are better for tasks that involve external services, like sending a notification or calling another API. Using the right tool for the job keeps your backend clean and efficient.

Is it Postgres or PostgreSQL? I keep seeing both.

Both are correct! The official name is PostgreSQL, but many people call it Postgres because it's simpler and less confusing. At Supabase, we use both interchangeably since the community is comfortable with both versions.

I heard I should use 'rules' for complex logic in my database. Is that right?

Actually, triggers are usually preferred over rules because they're easier to reason about and debug. Rules can rewrite your queries in unexpected ways, which often leads to confusion in complex schemas. Stick with triggers for a more predictable development experience.

Wait, is Supabase basically just a wrapper for Firebase?

Not at all! While both offer similar services, Supabase is built on top of PostgreSQL, a powerful relational database. Firebase uses a document-based NoSQL store, which is fundamentally different. We focus on giving you the best of the relational world with the ease of use you'd expect from a modern platform.

Does a database backup restore everything, including my images in Storage?

Important distinction: database backups only include your data and metadata, not the actual files in Storage. If you've deleted objects since the last backup, restoring the database won't bring those files back. Make sure you're thinking about both your DB and your object storage when planning your backups!

Can I just use the Supabase table view like an Excel spreadsheet?

It feels like a spreadsheet, but remember that it's a full Postgres database under the hood. While you can edit cells and add rows easily, you still have to follow database rules like data types and foreign key constraints. It's the best of both worlds: ease of use with industrial-strength stability.
